title: Chapter 14: Double Life
content: Smolder. Hold. Tilt the jaw three degrees left. Soften the eyes—no, not that soft, you're selling luxury, not vulnerability. There.
"My skin has never felt so alive."

I say it like I mean it. I say it like my soul isn't collateral on a demonic contract. The ring light catches the Valentino fabric draped across my collarbone—this shimmery, liquid thing that costs more than my mom's rent used to be—and I watch myself in the monitor behind the cameraman. Chloe Hart. Icy queen of content. Twenty-two million followers and a skincare deal that pays enough to fund a real lawyer, assuming I could find one who'd believe me.
"Beautiful," the director says. "We got it."

I hold the smile for thirty more seconds because the photographer wants stills. The Valentino shifts against my skin, cool and slippery as water, and I think about how the dress is a rental. Everything is a rental. My body, my face, the version of me that exists on other people's screens—I'm just leasing all of it from Mephisto Industries, and the lease terms are carved in something older than English.

The photographer thanks me. The director thanks me. The brand rep thanks me twice, because when you have twenty-two million followers, people thank you like you did them a personal favor by showing up.
I thank them back. Then I walk to the changing room and peel the Valentino off in under forty seconds. The paint-stained hoodie goes on in fifty. No mirror. No ring light. No softening of anything.
Ninety seconds flat. New record.
Same night, different species.

I'm hunched over my laptop at the kitchen table, the screen's blue light carving shadows under my eyes, and Leo's beside me with three monitors running code I can barely follow. The contract—all forty-seven pages of infernal legalese—is split between our screens, and we're reading it line by line by agonizing line.
"Clause 12.3," Leo says, scrolling. "It references 'binding arbitration as set forth in the Uniform Interstate Arbitration Act of 1923.'"
"Which doesn't exist anymore," I say.
"Which doesn't exist anymore," he echoes. He types something, pulls up a browser tab. "The UIAA was superseded by the Federal Arbitration Act amendments in—"

"1925. And then restructured again in the fifties." I'm already reaching for the marker. The black chisel-tip squeaks against the butcher paper I've taped across the entire living room wall. ARBITRATION → GHOST STATUTE, I write, and draw a line to connect it to three other annotations I made yesterday.

The wall is becoming something. Leo called it a conspiracy board, which, fair. But it's more like an art installation by a paranoid grad student. Handwritten notes in black and blue and red, connected by yards of red string I bought at a hardware store because the craft store was out. The string runs between pushpins in patterns that made sense at two in the morning and still make sense now, which is how I know I'm not crazy. Or at least not the wrong kind of crazy.

The contract's language is dense. Entertainment law on steroids, clauses nesting inside sub-clauses like Russian dolls made of fine print. But my eye keeps catching the same thing, over and over: references to legal frameworks that don't exist anymore. Dead laws. Zombie statutes.
"Here." I point at the screen. "Section 9. 'Pursuant to Section 14(b) of the Revised Commercial Code.' Leo, the Revised Commercial Code was replaced in 1952. This clause is pointing at a ghost."

Leo leans in. His eyes scan the text left to right, fast, the way only code people read. "It's not just this section. The whole contract is scaffolded on the 1923 framework. Every cross-reference, every dependency—they're all anchored to laws that got rewritten or tossed out decades ago."
"So when the law changed—"
"The contract didn't. It's still running on the old OS." He sits back. Looks at me. "Chloe, this thing is full of dead links."

I cap the marker. The squeak stops. In the silence, I can hear my own breathing, and under that, the soft hum of Leo's monitors, and under that, the city—always the city—doing its endless noise outside the window.
A contract full of dead links. A deal with the devil built on expired terms.
For the first time since this started, something like hope moves in my chest. Not warm. Not comfortable. More like a muscle I forgot I had, twitching back to life after years of atrophy.
I start testing it. Carefully. The way you'd poke a sleeping animal with a very long stick.

My Instagram story the next morning: a photo of the sunrise from my apartment window. Cranes silhouetted against orange sky. I slap a filter on it—something moody, blue undertones—and type the caption.
*Everything they tell you is permanent is temporary. Everything they say is temporary is yours forever.*
Post.
Within an hour, fourteen thousand likes. Comments like:
~aesthetic~
queen of vibes
is this about a breakup???
this hit different at 6am
poetry 🥺
My followers read it as ~aesthetic~. As mood. As content. And that's exactly what it is, on the surface. A pensive influencer being pensive. Brand consistency. Engagement bait.

But underneath? Underneath, it's a flare. A signal to anyone who might be watching that I see through the structure. Nothing is permanent—not the contract, not the laws it's built on, not the power Mephisto holds over me. And what's truly mine—my thoughts, my defiance, the part of me that hasn't been monetized—that's temporary only if I let them take it.

Mephisto's algorithm doesn't flag it. Of course it doesn't. The algorithm scans for keywords, for direct mentions, for breach-of-contract language. It doesn't understand metaphor. It can't read between the lines because there's no line-item value for "between the lines."
Leo looks at the post over my shoulder and lets out a short laugh. "You're hiding in plain sight."
"I'm a content creator," I say. "Hiding in plain sight is literally my job description."
He shakes his head, but he's smiling. That small, specific smile I'm starting to catalog—the one that means he's impressed and won't fully admit it.
I post again two days later. A mirror selfie. Hoodie up, no makeup, jaw set. Caption: *The face you show the world is the face the world eats. Feed it something indigestible.*
Forty thousand likes. A hundred think-piece tweets about self-care and authenticity. Zero flags from the algorithm.

I'm getting away with it. The realization lands somewhere between thrilling and terrifying. Every coded post is a tiny act of rebellion, a whispered heresy that the church of engagement metrics cannot parse. I am a heretic in a language the inquisitors don't speak.
It feels—God, it feels good.

The brand meeting is at a restaurant in SoHo. One of those places where the plates are white and enormous and the food is arranged like it's suing for attention. I sit across from an ad executive named—honestly, I zone out during the introduction—something with a hard C. Craig. Clark. He's got the kind of smile that's been professionally whitened and the kind of handshake that says he attended a seminar on power dynamics once.
"Synergy," he says, pointing a breadstick at me like a tiny scepter. "That's what this partnership is about. Synergy between your personal brand and our emotional resonance strategy."
I nod. I'm good at nodding. I've been nodding at men like him since I was seventeen.

"We've developed a targeting matrix that maps emotional response data to content verticals." He takes a bite of the breadstick. Crumbs fall on his tie. He doesn't notice. "So when your audience hits a specific emotional threshold—nostalgia, aspiration, vulnerability—we can serve ad content that integrates seamlessly with their psychological state."

The food arrives. Something with truffles. I don't eat it. The smell hits me first—rich, earthy, almost aggressively fancy—and then the taste when I take a courtesy bite is fine, I guess. Truffle oil and something green and a texture that's supposed to be luxurious but just feels slimy on my tongue. I put my fork down.

"We're not just selling skincare," he continues. He's leaning forward now, really committed. "We're selling the *feeling* that skincare gives you. The confidence. The sense of self-worth. And your followers trust you to curate that feeling for them."
Harvest emotional data. Map psychological states. Serve targeted content based on vulnerability windows.

I'm sitting across from a man in a Tom Ford suit who is describing—in the jargon of modern advertising—exactly what Mephisto does. The playbook is the same. The methodology is identical. The difference is scale. Craig-or-Clark harvests engagement metrics to sell moisturizer. Mephisto harvests emotional data to consume souls. But the machinery? Same gears. Same logic.

Capitalism and Hell use the same playbook. I want to laugh, but laughing would break character, and breaking character would break the meeting, and breaking the meeting would mean explaining to my agent why the skincare deal fell through. So I smile and say, "That sounds amazing. I'm really excited about the synergy."
The word tastes like the truffle oil. Slimy. Expensive. Fake.
His cologne—sandalwood and something sweeter, jasmine maybe—hangs between us like a third person at the table. I breathe through my mouth for the rest of the meeting.
Back home, Leo's found something.

I know it the second I walk in because he's got that energy—the one where his leg won't stop bouncing and his fingers are already talking before his mouth catches up. Three monitors blazing. Code scrolling on two of them, the contract on the third.
"Sit down," he says. Not rude. Urgent.
I sit.
"The contract has a self-repair mechanism."
My stomach drops. "What."

"Listen—when a clause gets challenged or a loophole gets exploited, the contract auto-generates a patch. It rewrites the compromised section to close the gap." He pulls up a visualization—some kind of flowchart he's built, arrows and nodes in green and red. "See these? These are patch events. Every time someone's tried to fight this thing, it's healed itself."
"So we're screwed."

"No. That's the thing—" He swivels to face me. His eyes are bright. Tired, but bright. "The patches are built on the 1923 framework too. Every repair, every update, every fix the contract makes—it's using the same dead statutes. The same ghost laws. It can't update the foundation without a full rewrite, and a full rewrite requires—"
"The Creator's consent," I finish.

"The Creator's consent. Which means Mephisto can't fix the bug without going back to the source. Without rewriting the entire contract from scratch. And to do that, he'd have to notify you. Renegotiate. Re-open the deal."
I look at the wall. The red string. The handwritten annotations. The conspiracy board that isn't a conspiracy if it's true.
"He's stuck," I say slowly. "He's got a self-healing contract that heals wrong. He can't patch it properly without alerting me, and alerting me means I'd know exactly where to look."
"Catch-22." Leo's leg has stopped bouncing. He's calm now, the way you get calm after you've been running and finally see the road ahead. "We have a window."

"A closing window," I say, because I can't help it. I've never been able to leave a thing unsaid. "The patches still work short-term. Every time we exploit a loophole, it closes. We get one shot per vulnerability."
"One shot is more than we had yesterday."
He's right. He's right, and I know he's right, and the hope is back—twitching, uncomfortable, insistent.
But so is the clock.
I go to bed but I don't sleep.

The double life is exhausting in a way I can't name. It's not the hours—though the hours are brutal, filming all day and researching all night. It's the switching. The shape-shifting. Being Chloe HartTM on camera, smile dialed to eleven, selling skincare and confidence and the lie that consumption equals self-care. Then becoming Chloe Hart, lowercase, off-camera, in a paint-stained hoodie, reading demonic contracts with a boy who believes me without question. The whiplash would be bad enough on its own. But it's not just the speed of the switch. It's the distance. Like the two versions of me exist on different planets, and I'm the only bridge between them, and the bridge is starting to crack.

I stare at my phone. The screen fills the dark room with soft blue light. Twenty-two million followers. The number ticked up again today. I watched it happen in real time—five thousand new followers in an hour—and every new one felt like a poker chip being added to a stack I didn't ask to play.

The 25M milestone is coming. This is the one that takes nostalgia. When I hit twenty-five million followers, the contract deducts my ability to feel nostalgic. Memories won't disappear—not yet—but their emotional weight will. The warmth. The ache. The bittersweet thing that makes remembering worth doing.

I can already feel the edges getting fuzzy. Not the memories themselves, but the way they land. Like frost forming on a window. You can still see through it, but everything's dimmer. Colder. Flattened.

I tried to remember my mom's kitchen this morning—specifically, the way sunlight hit the yellow walls in the afternoon—and I could see it, but I couldn't feel it. The warmth was data without sensation. A description of heat instead of heat.
Three million more followers. That's all that stands between me and a world where nothing I remember matters.
In the other room, Leo's typing has slowed. I hear him yawn—a long, jaw-cracking sound that travels through the thin wall. Then the click of his laptop closing. Then quiet.
He's still here. That matters. He's still here, and he's still fighting, and we have a window.
But the window is closing.

The self-repair mechanism means we have to be fast. Every exploit is single-use. Every loophole seals behind us. We get one path through the maze, and if we choose wrong, the walls heal over and we're trapped again.

Mephisto can't fix the foundation. But he doesn't need to. He just needs the patches to hold long enough for me to hit the next milestone. And the next. And the next. Until there's nothing left of me to fight with.

I put the phone down. The screen goes dark. The room goes dark. The red string on the wall is invisible now, but I can still see it when I close my eyes—a web of connections, a map of dead laws and ghost statutes, a plan that might work if we're fast enough and smart enough and lucky enough.
If.
The frost is on the window. The clock is ticking. And the double life is not sustainable—but then, nothing about this is sustainable, and sustainability has never been the point.
Survival is the point.
I close my eyes and listen to the quiet and try not to count followers, try not to count milestones, try not to count the ways this can go wrong.
I fail.
